question: Compare the NuScale vs typical PWR vessel dimensions from Table 1.3-1 with the detailed RPV specifications in Table 5.3-1.

nuscale_ch01_0558.title: DSRS 12.3-12.4, Rev 0: Radiation Protection Design FeaturesII.9 RG 8.15
nuscale_ch01_0558.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with various regulatory guides related to radiation protection design features as outlined in DSRS 12.3-12.4, Rev 0. It details whether each regulatory guide is applicable, not applicable, or only partially conformed to, with references to supporting sections and tables for further information. Most guides are either not applicable or partially conformed to, with only a few fully met.

nuscale_ch05_0014.title: Reactor Vessel
nuscale_ch05_0014.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module's reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single vessel surrounded by a containment vessel. The RPV serves as a critical barrier to fission product release and supports various reactor components, with its materials and fabrication adhering to stringent ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code standards to ensure long-term integrity and safety.

nuscale_ch05_0017.title: Reactor Vessel Integrity
nuscale_ch05_0017.summary: This section outlines the procedures and regulatory compliance measures to ensure the integrity of the NuScale reactor vessel over its 60-year design life. It details the development of operating procedures to maintain pressure-temperature limits, evaluates the vessel’s resistance to pressurized thermal shock and upper-shelf energy degradation, and confirms that predicted material properties remain within safety criteria throughout the vessel’s operational lifespan.

nuscale_ch01_0033.title: General Arrangement of Major Structures and Equipment
nuscale_ch01_0033.summary: This section describes the general arrangement of major structures and equipment at a NuScale Power Plant, focusing on the reactor building (RXB). The RXB is centrally located, constructed of reinforced concrete for safety and protection, and houses the NuScale Power Modules (NPMs) in a common reactor pool arranged in two rows. The section also references detailed figures and chapters for further information on radioactive waste management and building layouts.

nuscale_ch01_0052.title: Comparison with Other Facilities
nuscale_ch01_0052.summary: This section compares the major design features and parameters of the NuScale Power Plant with those of a typical pressurized water reactor (PWR). It provides side-by-side data on aspects such as electrical output, core design, reactor coolant system, and safety components, highlighting the differences in scale, configuration, and safety system requirements between NuScale’s small modular reactor and conventional PWRs. The comparison is intended for reference and uses nominal values for both designs.

nuscale_ch05_0013.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0013.summary: This section lists references and standards used in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report regarding the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary. It includes guidelines and reports from the Electric Power Research Institute and the American Society of Mechanical Engineers, as well as tables summarizing relevant ASME code cases for reactor components.

nuscale_ch05_0011.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Inservice Inspection and Testing
nuscale_ch05_0011.summary: This section describes the materials and standards used for threaded fasteners in key reactor coolant system components, emphasizing the use of Alloy 718 for its compatibility and stress corrosion cracking resistance. It also outlines the requirements for preservice and inservice inspection and testing of reactor coolant pressure boundary components, specifying adherence to the latest ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) editions as mandated by regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance across various power levels by establishing steady-state parameters for both primary and secondary systems, such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and water levels. These parameters are calculated using thermal hydraulic software and verified by hand, with uncertainties and design margins considered to ensure efficient operation and safety. The design supports natural circulation with minimal pressure losses, and uncertainties related to steam generator tube plugging do not affect primary coolant flow rate determination.

nuscale_ch01_0817.title: DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0: Thermal Hydraulic Stability Review ResponsibilitiesII.11 D&S System extremely high probability of functioning in the event of an AOO.
nuscale_ch01_0817.summary: This section reviews NuScale’s conformance with DSRS 15.9.A requirements for thermal hydraulic stability. The NuScale design does not use detect and suppress (D&S) methods or exclusion zones; instead, reactor trip signals act preemptively to prevent conditions that could lead to flow instabilities, making additional D&S systems unnecessary. The existing Reactor Trip System (RTS) is considered sufficient to ensure safety by preventing violation of safety limits before instability can develop.

nuscale_ch01_0818.title: DSRS 16.0, Rev. 0: Technical SpecificationsAll (No Number)Acceptance Criteria for Technical Specifications
nuscale_ch01_0818.summary: This section explains that while the NuScale Design Certification Application (DCA) partially conforms to DSRS 16.0 acceptance criteria for technical specifications, much of the specific language is tailored to existing or future plant-specific requirements to be developed by COL applicants. The DCA includes proposed technical specifications based on relevant regulations and industry guidance, with technical justifications provided for any differences from standard specifications.

edges.description: Section A references regulatory conformance and general design features, while Section B provides the detailed reactor pressure vessel (RPV) specifications that concretely define the vessel dimensions and configuration, thus Section B specifies the technical details underlying the general descriptions in Section A.

Section A references regulatory guidance and conformance tables that set general requirements for reactor vessel design, while Section B provides specific operating procedures and compliance measures for the NuScale reactor vessel, thus Section B specifies how the general requirements in Section A are implemented for the reactor vessel.

Section A references regulatory guidance and conformance statements, while Section B provides the general arrangement and system descriptions; both sections are structurally related as Section A cross-references the detailed system descriptions and figures found in Section B to demonstrate regulatory conformance.

Section B's Table 1.3-1 provides comparative vessel dimension values for NuScale and typical PWRs, which are further detailed and specified by the RPV specifications in Section A's Table 5.3-1, allowing for a direct comparison of general and detailed design parameters.

Section A provides the physical and design context of the NuScale reactor pressure vessel, while Section B specifies the operational procedures and requirements that ensure the vessel's integrity is maintained within those design limits.

Section A provides detailed specifications of the NuScale reactor pressure vessel (RPV), while Section B offers a general overview and layout of major plant structures, with Section A specifying the technical details that elaborate on the general descriptions in Section B.

Section A provides the detailed reactor pressure vessel (RPV) specifications for NuScale, while Section B presents a comparative summary table (Table 1.3-1) that includes NuScale and typical PWR vessel dimensions, connecting them through the vessel dimension data used for comparison.

Section A discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel integrity by not exceeding pressure-temperature limits, which relies on the vessel dimensions and specifications detailed in Section B's general arrangement and referenced tables.

Section B provides nominal vessel dimension values for both NuScale and typical PWRs in Table 1.3-1, which can be directly compared to the detailed NuScale RPV specifications found in Section A's Table 5.3-1, allowing for a quantitative analysis of design differences.

Section B's Table 1.3-1 provides comparative vessel dimensions for NuScale and typical PWRs, which can be directly cross-checked against the detailed RPV specifications found in Table 5.3-1 referenced in Section A.

Section A provides references and standards that underpin the technical basis for reactor vessel design, while Section B outlines the regulatory criteria and conformance statements that the vessel specifications (such as those in Table 1.3-1 and Table 5.3-1) must satisfy, connecting the vessel dimensions and specifications to regulatory compliance.

Section A provides references and standards that underpin the technical and regulatory basis for the reactor vessel specifications detailed in Section B, connecting the cited guidelines and requirements to the vessel's design and description.

Section A provides references and standards that underpin the technical and regulatory basis for reactor vessel integrity, while Section B describes the implementation of these standards through operating procedures to ensure vessel integrity is maintained within specified limits.

Section A provides detailed material and design requirements for RPV threaded fasteners, while Section B lists regulatory guidance documents and the degree of NuScale's conformance, connecting them through the regulatory standards that Section A's specifications are intended to satisfy.

Section A references the design and material specifications for RPV threaded fasteners, while Section B provides a general description of the RPV and its role in the reactor coolant system, with both sections connected through their focus on RPV components and their compliance with regulatory and design requirements.

Section A specifies the material and design standards for RPV threaded fasteners, which are part of the detailed RPV specifications referenced in Table 5.3-1, while Section B discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure vessel integrity, both relying on the vessel's design values such as those compared in Table 1.3-1 and detailed in Table 5.3-1.

Section A provides references to industry standards and guidelines that are used to justify or support the material selection and quality assurance requirements described in Section B for the RPV threaded fasteners.

Section A references the system evaluation criteria and performance parameters for the NuScale Power Plant, while Section B provides the detailed description and specifications of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), with both sections connected through their discussion of the RPV's role and characteristics as established in the referenced tables.

Section A discusses the optimization and performance criteria for the NuScale reactor system, which are directly influenced by the reactor vessel dimensions detailed in Section B, as these specifications set the physical and operational constraints for system evaluation.

Section A references Table 1.3-1 to compare NuScale and typical PWR vessel dimensions, while Section B provides the detailed RPV specifications from Table 5.3-1, allowing a direct comparison of design values between the two tables.

Section B specifies that operating procedures will ensure pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded, which supports Section A's claim that protective actions prevent violation of critical heat flux (CHF) limits before unstable flow oscillations can develop.

Section A discusses the acceptance criteria for technical specifications, which include requirements for vessel dimensions as referenced in Table 1.3-1, while Section B addresses radiation protection design features that are influenced by the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) specifications detailed in Table 5.3-1, thus connecting the vessel dimensions to their impact on radiation protection design.

Section A references the need for plant-specific technical specifications, while Section B provides detailed reactor pressure vessel (RPV) specifications that would be used to develop those technical specifications.

Section B describes the development of operating procedures to ensure compliance with technical specifications referenced in Section A, specifically linking the vessel's operational limits and requirements to the acceptance criteria outlined in Section A.

Section A references vessel dimensions in Table 1.3-1 for NuScale and typical PWRs, which are then specified in greater detail in Section B's Table 5.3-1, connecting general comparative data to detailed design specifications.

Section A provides system evaluation and performance criteria for the NuScale reactor, while Section B outlines the regulatory acceptance criteria for technical specifications, connecting them through the need for NuScale's vessel dimensions and specifications (from Table 1.3-1 and Table 5.3-1) to satisfy the regulatory requirements described in Section B.

Section A discusses the NuScale design's approach to thermal hydraulic stability and reactor protection, while Section B outlines the acceptance criteria for technical specifications, with both sections connected by their focus on ensuring regulatory compliance for plant safety systems, particularly regarding how design features and technical specifications must align to meet NRC requirements.
